nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing and moderately flowing rivers and streams; rocky substrates, submerged roots, and driftwood. Inhabits a range of conditions from clear water over gravel to more turbid environments.
nativeRange.biotope: Amazon and Paraná river basins; South American rivers with driftwood and rocky cover

decorPreferences: driftwood (rasps biofilm and cellulose from wood surface)
caves and PVC tubes for shelter and breeding

diet.notes: Primarily herbivorous in practice — algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, green beans, cucumber), and biofilm on driftwood make up the bulk of the diet. Driftwood is strongly recommended as a dietary supplement; Ancistrus rasp cellulose and biofilm from wood surfaces, which is thought to benefit digestive health. Supplementing with protein-rich foods such as frozen bloodworms or Repashy gel food a few times per week rounds out the diet.

avoidWith: other bottom-dwelling plecos competing for territory
fin-nipping species when keeping long-fin morphs (tiger barbs, serpae tetras)
very small fish that may be harassed

imageCandidates.notes: EnjiBenji, 1774x1356. Sharp mature male on a breeding cave in a planted tank showing the diagnostic bushy snout bristles and honeycomb/reticulated pattern; quintessential bristlenose look. CC0.
JanRehschuh, 1569x1044. Ancistrus dolichopterus male with dramatic dense snout bristles fully displayed; crisp, shows the defining fleshy tentacles of the genus.
Uploader, 3872x2592. Ancistrus temminckii male, whole fish in frame on gravel with visible snout bristles; adds a distinct species to the genus set.
The Last 99, 1024x682. Ancistrus cirrhosus, dark body with vivid gold spotting; colourful representative of another named species (image looks slightly enhanced but sharp and correctly ID'd).
DENker, 3543x2362. Tack-sharp side profile, olive body with golden spots and raised dorsal (attributed to A. triradiatus on iNaturalist); highest-clarity full-body shot in the set.
Andre Karwath (Aka), 2946x1035. Clean sharp wild-type Ancistrus sp., whole fish with dorsal raised over gravel; classic mottled brown appearance.
Billyhill, 3264x1442. Group of three Ancistrus sp. in a planted aquarium; sharp foreground fish, shows how the fish look together in the hobby.
Corydoras-adolfoi, 3008x2000. Golden/xanthic long-fin L144 captive morph on sand; sharp, whole fish, documents a widely-sold aquarium colour form.

summary: A small, hardy pleco from South American river systems, widely regarded as one of the most beginner-friendly members of the Loricariidae. Males develop distinctive fleshy bristles on the snout. Excellent algae eater and easy to breed in caves.
careNotes:
Several Ancistrus species are sold in the hobby under the Bristlenose Pleco name, most commonly A. cirrhosus and A. dolichopterus along with various L-number forms. Care requirements are broadly consistent across the genus for the species commonly seen in the aquarium trade. Albino and long-fin captive-bred morphs are also widely available and share the same care requirements; long-fin individuals should be kept away from fin-nipping tankmates.

Males tend to be territorial toward one another and toward other bottom-dwelling catfish; providing one cave or shelter per male is generally advised. Females are generally more tolerant of conspecifics, and a ratio of more females than males is recommended for groups.

Driftwood is strongly recommended in the setup — Ancistrus have been documented rasping biofilm and cellulose from wood surfaces, which is thought to contribute to digestive health. A smooth rock or driftwood surface is preferred over sharp substrate.

As with most catfishes, Ancistrus are reported to be sensitive to copper-based medications. Copper-free treatments should be used, or the fish removed from the display tank before any copper-containing medication is added.

Active primarily at dusk and dawn (crepuscular), the Bristlenose Pleco tends to be most visible when the lights are dim or off. Feeding after lights dim often yields the best results. It is not strictly nocturnal, however, and may be observed during daytime hours, particularly in well-planted or shaded tanks.

Cross-reference: [common-pleco] (Pterygoplichthys pardalis) for the much larger relative often confused at juvenile stage; [clown-pleco] (Panaqolus maccus) for a similarly sized wood-eating sibling.
breedingNotes: Generally considered one of the easiest aquarium fish to breed. Cave-spawner: the male selects and defends a cave (clay pots, PVC tubes, or coconut shells work well), entices the female inside, and she deposits 20–80 adhesive orange eggs. The male guards and fans the clutch until hatching (4–7 days at 26°C). Fry are free-swimming within a few more days and can be fed algae wafers, blanched vegetables, and fine sinking foods. A 20-gallon long or equivalent tank is often cited as sufficient for a breeding pair. Males may become more aggressive toward tankmates while guarding eggs.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (10–13 cm), temperature (23–26°C), pH (5.7–7.8), gH (2–20), minimum tank (113 L / 30 gal), lifespan (5–8 yr, up to 12+). Good agreement with primary on all high-stakes fields.

additional.name: FishBase — Ancistrus dolichopterus
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Loricariidae / Siluriformes), native range (Amazon / Negro / Madeira / Tapajós basins), max size 14.5 cm SL, temperature 23–27°C, pH 6.0–8.0, dH 5–19. Used to verify parameters and anchor upper size bound for genus entry.

additional.name: FishBase — Ancistrus cirrhosus
additional.notes: Max size 9.1 cm SL (Paraná basin); confirms Loricariidae / Siluriformes; facultative air-breathing noted. Used to anchor lower size bound and confirm taxonomy.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner; the male guards eggs and fry. Breeds readily in captivity with basic cave furnishings and is widely regarded as one of the most easily bred aquarium catfishes.
